Zacharias used to talk to physicians weight loss centers about the moving credit card blight in America that settles over one city at a time each month where nobody is able to sell any weight loss programs, but every place else everybody is doing fine. Well, that's sort of like the survivor walk of shame across the bridge when you've been voted out at council, right? I mean, I like that. That's good. You can actually frame and put the photos of the losers up on the wall, right? Or the departed hall of fame. I like that. As far as ways of confronting the elephants, although my belief is that once you identify them, you've got to confront them as many different ways as you can. I made a statement sitting at the table. I was wondering what your thoughts are about it. The whole notion of really the best way to confront the elephants is to have it through testimonial where other people are telling their story and it's alluded to in the story and how they dealt with that. So like, I was thinking about, or a continuity program, I was thinking about all I'd do is stay for like the free trial period and dropout right afterwards, but you know, da da da da. Do you think that's still the overall most powerful way to deal with it? Yeah. I think that if it's not the only way that you dealt with it in a particular place and time, it ought to be one of the ways you dealt with it in that particular place and time. In terms of making impact and believability, I mean, it goes back to the premise of the testimony itself when others say it's much more believable than you say by yourself. I can't imagine a place where you wouldn't use it, assuming you had one, but I wouldn't rely on them exclusively. I think you should do it everywhere you can, including in your own copy, but I would think that that would be like the most powerful of the ways to address it. See, like even if you take their extreme example, so if you had the hull of the departed, you know, the best second punchline to that would be somebody who had left and come back, who stepped to the stage and said, you know, I was on that wall a year ago and I went back out into the hinterlands and realized how wrong I was and now I'm back in the fold and let me tell you what's happened to me. I mean, that would be the best of all punchlines, you know, to that impact. I've got one which is liability because of the nature of our products. People are always asking about liability and answer it that way. In 20 years of selling these products, there's never been one lawsuit against safety technology or any of our distributors. Anybody who might sue us typically dies. So there's been some deaths, but there's been no litigation. No, I agree. And I agree in your business that like ignoring that and hoping they're not thinking it, losing proposition. Charlie, you want it in? No. Okay. 137. One thing is that the elephants that we talked about, the idea that occurred to me is how does this any different than an objection, but an elephant is the big unspoken one that nobody wants to really talk about that you really should. Yeah. See, in face-to-face selling, although it's a mistake to assume it all the time, but generally in a face-to-face sales situation, so like if you're doing a case presentation, right, the person has an opportunity to play this game with us. So they have an opportunity to raise their concern, and we have an opportunity then to deal with it. When we're working with media, obviously they don't have that opportunity. So it's not a matter of an objection because they don't even get to raise the objection. They raise it. It's just there's nobody there to answer it. And so it is about, it is the raise the objection and answer it. But then it goes a little bit deeper than that because there are elephants that people, that are affecting people that they don't even enunciate, right, that we may want to enunciate for them. So like if you go back to Carl Stokes, see there's hardly anybody. So like right now in Obama's case, there's hardly anybody who will admit to, I won't vote for this guy because he's black. There's hardly anybody who will admit to, I won't vote for Hillary because I don't want a woman to be president. That's so unenlightened that nobody wants to verbalize it. Yeah. But, well let me say it another way. There are a whole lot more people who think it, who will never enunciate it, would never raise it as an objection, you know, would never voice it. But it is a mistake on both their parts not to address it. Because it's in the way, right? And so see like in your case, so like the vanity thing. There are a lot of people who would never, and they might even not be totally conscious of what's going on, but they would never say to you, you know, I don't want to spend money on this because, you know, my best girlfriend or my family's going to think I'm horribly vain. But you and I know that gets in the way. So better to trot it out and make it go away than to ignore it. So this is sort of bigger than just the sort of common list of objections that people, so like if you got to this list by simply talking to your five best sales people and what are the eight objections everybody raises and now we're going to go answer the objections in a sales letter. That's like the narrow application of this. But it doesn't get us to the objections nobody ever raises, right? It doesn't get us deeper than what goes on in an interactive conversation between salesperson and prospect. And so like see what I saw, like what I saw in the Miracle-Ear transcripts like 50% of the time. And so if you got the information from the sales people they would tell you that people were raising price objections. And they answered them as price objections. And you would then go create copy to make price objections go away. But since the sales people were misreading what the objection was and not understanding really what the person was thinking versus what they were saying, if you go back to the culture code thing, one of the brilliant things that he says in there is the only way to totally understand a customer is to ignore what they're saying. Now of course that's exact opposite of sort of standard focus group wisdom. But in the Miracle-Ear case what the customer was saying in about half of those transcripts that was perceived as a price objection was really the legacy objection. I feel guilty spending $8,000 on me when my son and daughter in law are struggling to pay for their house and I got five grandkids and so better I should keep the money in a drawer and have it for them when I pass away. Now he didn't enunciate it but that really is what was going on. And so it's not just the stated and the obvious objections, it's the deeper psychological stuff. Page 137. Here's a...
